calendar.matters:
MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Feb-25-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DENESHA BRIDGES' MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT AND VACATE JUDGMENT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to set aside default and vacate judgment is granted.

"[C]ompliance with the statutory procedures for service of process is essential to establish personal jurisdiction. Thus, a default judgment entered against a defendant who was not served with a summons in the manner prescribed by statute is void. Under [Code of Civil Procedure] section 473, subdivision (d), the court may set aside a default judgment which is valid on its face, but void, as a matter of law, due to improper service." (Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 544.) Relief from a judgment that is valid on its face but void for improper service must be sought within 2 years of entry of the default judgment. (Rogers v. Silverman (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 1114, 1126.)

Defendant declares that she was never personally served in this action - that at the time of service she lived at a different address and that "Plaintiff allegedly served the Complaint to someone they claim is me but was not me." (Mt., pg. 4, 4.) Further, Defendant declares that she only became aware of this action when "reviewing a different legal matter." (Id. at 3.) Defendant declares that she moved to Sacramento in 2006 and provides an interim license issued June 1, 2012 and 2012 homeowner documents addressed to her on 79th Street in Sacramento (Bridges Decl., Ex. 1.) While Plaintiff's factual showing calls into question the veracity of Defendant's statements, "[i]t is the policy of the law to favor, whenever possible, a hearing on the merits . . . Therefore, when a party in default moves promptly to seek relief, very slight evidence is required to justify a trial court's order setting aside a default. (Shamblin v. Brattain (1988) 44 Cal.3d 474, 478.) The Court also notes that the complaint, which was filed more than three years after the underlying accident, appears to be time-barred on its face. (See Commercial Union Assurance Co. v. City of San Jose (1982) 127 Cal.App.3d 730, 735 [subrogor insurer's right to recover on insured's cause of action was barred when statutory period ran on insured's claim].) Thus, Defendant appears to have a meritorious defense to the complaint.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)
